skill.name: frontend-accessibility
skill.description: Guides frontend work toward accessible HTML, keyboard support, focus management, color contrast, labels, ARIA usage, and screen reader compatibility.
skill.summary: Build accessible React and web interfaces with practical WCAG-oriented guidance.
skill.author: DevanB

security_audit.summary: Static shell execution and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown text and ordinary words. The Markdown link on line 29 uses parent-directory traversal to reference guidance outside the packaged skill, which creates a medium documentation and filesystem-boundary risk.

medium_findings.title: External Relative Documentation Reference
medium_findings.description: Line 29 links to ../../../agent-os/standards/frontend/accessibility.md, which points outside the skill package. This is not executable code, but it can cause the skill to depend on unavailable or unintended files in a host repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory traversal is directly visible in a Markdown link. The risk is bounded because it is documentation, not code execution or automatic file access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Inline Code Detected as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Line 12 mentions .tsx and .jsx file extensions inside Markdown backticks. There is no Ruby code, shell command, command substitution, or user-controlled execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious characters are Markdown formatting around file extensions. No executable syntax or command runner appears in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Lines 3 and 8 contain prose about accessibility guidance. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, cipher, or security-sensitive implementation appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are natural-language metadata and description text. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Parent Directory Traversal in Skill Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references a file outside its own directory through ../../../. This can make marketplace behavior depend on repository layout outside the packaged skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The traversal sequence is explicit on the cited line. It is concerning for packaging integrity, although not malicious by itself.

content.value_statement: Frontend teams often miss accessibility requirements during component work. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for accessible UI implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guides semantic HTML choices for page structure and interactive controls.
Prompts for labels, alternative text, headings, and landmark regions.
Supports keyboard navigation, focus indicators, and focus management planning.
Encourages WCAG color contrast checks for text and interface states.
Recommends ARIA attributes for complex patterns when native HTML is insufficient.
Highlights screen reader testing as part of frontend review.
limitations: It provides guidance, not automated accessibility testing.
The referenced external accessibility file is not included in this package.
It does not guarantee legal compliance with accessibility regulations.
It focuses on frontend UI work, not backend accessibility concerns.

use_cases.title: Create accessible React components
use_cases.description: Use the skill while building forms, buttons, dialogs, menus, and other interactive components.

use_cases.title: Review UI changes before merge
use_cases.description: Ask the assistant to check labels, focus order, contrast, headings, and ARIA use before code review.

use_cases.title: Improve design implementation handoff
use_cases.description: Convert visual requirements into accessible frontend behavior with keyboard and screen reader considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Check a component for accessibility
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one frontend component.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this component for accessibility. Focus on semantic HTML, labels, keyboard support, focus states, and screen reader behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Build an accessible form
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a form with clear accessible behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an accessible form for this workflow. Include labels, validation messaging, error association, keyboard behavior, and visible focus states.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an interactive pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Refine a modal, menu, tab list, or dialog.
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this interactive UI pattern for accessibility. Address focus trapping, escape behavior, ARIA roles, announcements, and keyboard navigation.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a frontend flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across several screens or components.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this frontend flow against WCAG-oriented accessibility practices. Prioritize blocking issues, explain impact, and propose specific implementation changes.

output_examples.input: A login form has placeholder-only labels and generic error text.
output: Add persistent labels for each input.
Connect each error message to its input.
Use clear error text that explains the required correction.
Keep keyboard focus visible during validation.

output_examples.input: A modal opens but focus stays behind the dialog.
output: Move focus to the dialog when it opens.
Keep tab focus inside the dialog until it closes.
Return focus to the opener after close.
Support Escape to close when appropriate.

output_examples.input: A dashboard uses low-contrast status colors without text.
output: Add text labels for each status.
Increase contrast for text and controls.
Avoid using color as the only status signal.
Verify hover and focus states meet contrast needs.

best_practices: Start with native HTML elements before adding ARIA.
Test every interactive control with only a keyboard.
Check color contrast for default, hover, focus, and disabled states.
anti_patterns: Using div elements as buttons without keyboard support.
Adding ARIA roles that conflict with native element semantics.
Relying on color alone to communicate errors or status.

faq.question: Does this skill run accessibility tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides implementation and review, but separate tools should run automated checks.

faq.question: Can it help with React components?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explicitly targets React component files and common frontend UI patterns.

faq.question: Does it replace manual screen reader testing?
faq.answer: No. It encourages screen reader testing, but human validation is still required.

faq.question: When should I use ARIA?
faq.answer: Use ARIA when native HTML cannot express the needed role, state, or relationship.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning about an external relative documentation reference.

faq.question: What should maintainers improve?
faq.answer: They should include the referenced accessibility guidance inside the skill package.
